coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot modified Bi2MoO6 nanoflower for efficient degradation of BPA under visible light

coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a novel type of carbon dot material that has significant application value in the field of catalysis due to their non-toxic, stable, abundant surface functional groups, and quantum confinement effects. A unique composite photocatalyst was constructed by modifying GQDs onto Bi2MoO6 (BMO) microsphere-shaped nano petals using simple hydrothermal and sintering techniques. The structural and morphological characterization results indicate that GQDs with the size of 10 nm are well dispersed on BMO nanosheets, forming close contacts, which can greatly improve the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation. In the evaluation of the catalytic performance of BPA solution (20 mg/L) with a catalyst content of 20 mg under a simulated light source with a power of 300 W, the best degradation performance was achieved by a photocatalyst (G6/BMO) with the GQDs mass ratio of 6%, which degraded over 95% of BPA under visible light within 120 min, while pure BMO only degraded about 45% of BPA during the same time period. Even if the 400 nm filter is removed and directly exposed to Xe lamp radiation, the degradation performance of the optimal composite catalyst is only slightly improved, indicating that the current GQDs/BMO composite catalyst has extremely strong visible light catalytic activity. The improvement of catalytic performance comes from the effective separation of electron-hole pairs caused by the absorption of electrons by GQDs, and the introduction of GQDs to reduce the band gap and enhance visible light absorption, both of which are beneficial for catalytic reactions. Free radical capture and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests indicate that ·OH and ·O2 − are the main active species for BPA degradation. Although the current GQDs/BMO catalysts have a simple structure, their catalytic performance has significantly improved, which will guide the design of other semiconductor based photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot modified Bi2MoO6 nanoflower for efficient degradation of BPA under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a novel type of carbon dot material that has significant application value in the field of catalysis due to their non-toxic, stable, abundant surface functional groups, and quantum confinement effects. A unique composite photocatalyst was constructed by modifying GQDs onto Bi2MoO6 (BMO) microsphere-shaped nano petals using simple hydrothermal and sintering techniques. The structural and morphological characterization results indicate that GQDs with the size of 10 nm are well dispersed on BMO nanosheets, forming close contacts, which can greatly improve the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation. In the evaluation of the catalytic performance of BPA solution (20 mg/L) with a catalyst content of 20 mg under a simulated light source with a power of 300 W, the best degradation performance was achieved by a photocatalyst (G6/BMO) with the GQDs mass ratio of 6%, which degraded over 95% of BPA under visible light within 120 min, while pure BMO only degraded about 45% of BPA during the same time period. Even if the 400 nm filter is removed and directly exposed to Xe lamp radiation, the degradation performance of the optimal composite catalyst is only slightly improved, indicating that the current GQDs/BMO composite catalyst has extremely strong visible light catalytic activity. The improvement of catalytic performance comes from the effective separation of electron-hole pairs caused by the absorption of electrons by GQDs, and the introduction of GQDs to reduce the band gap and enhance visible light absorption, both of which are beneficial for catalytic reactions. Free radical capture and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests indicate that ·OH and ·O2 − are the main active species for BPA degradation. Although the current GQDs/BMO catalysts have a simple structure, their catalytic performance has significantly improved, which will guide the design of other semiconductor based photocatalysts.

A composite photocatalyst with simple structure consisting of GQDs modified BMO nanoflower has been obtained, which exhibits excellent visible light driven photocatalytic activity.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Plastic products play an important role in our daily lives, covering everything from tableware to containers to various other industrial products [1–3]. However, bisphenol A (BPA) commonly found in plastic products is a harmful chemical to the human body. BPA has estrogenic activity and can interfere with the endocrine system, increasing the risk of cancer, reproductive system diseases, and other illnesses. In July 2024, the European Union notified the World Trade Organization (WTO) of its draft regulation prohibiting the use of BPA and related substances in specific food contact materials and products. The ban indicates that the harm of BPA cannot be ignored, and it is urgent to implement harmless degradation treatment for it.

Photocatalysis is considered for the purification of toxic organic pollutants due to its high degradation efficiency and environmental friendliness [4–6]. Wide bandgap semiconductor photocatalysts, represented by TiO2, are widely used in energy and environmental protection fields due to their high catalytic activity, good chemical stability, non toxicity to the human body, and low cost, and are considered as the most important photocatalysts. However, the wide bandgap (3.2 eV) results in a lower utilization efficiency of TiO2 for sunlight, which greatly limits its application in photocatalysis [7]. The light absorption capacity, energy level, and the generation, recombination [8–10], separation, migration [11–15], and capture of photogenerated carriers of catalysts directly affect the photocatalytic efficiency [15–19]. Therefore, the development of semiconductor catalysts with high carrier mobility driven by visible light has become a goal pursued by researchers in recent years [20–23].

Bi2MoO6 (BMO) has a suitable band gap of around 2.7 eV at room temperature and exhibits visible light catalytic activity [24,25]. Its unique crystal and electronic structures are considered to be a candidate for the degradation of BPA. However, due to electron-hole recombination, the performance of pure BMO is not satisfactory. To achieve better catalytic effects, the most common strategies include electrocatalysis [26], conjugated polymers [27], defect state engineering [28], organic framework construction [29] and heterostructure construction [30–35]. The Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2023 is awarded to scientists who have made outstanding contributions to the discovery and synthesis of quantum dots (QDs) [36], making 0D QDs with special physical, chemical, and optical function star materials. In fact, QDs not only have excellent luminescent properties, but also perform well in the field of catalysis [37,38]. Kong et al. prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-decorated ultrathin Bi2WO6 nanosheets for increasing photocatalytic efficiency [39]. The addition of CQDs resulted in an optimal CH4 yield of 7.19 μmol g−1 h−1. We have successfully synthesized boron doped carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) modified BMO nanoflower shaped microsphere photocatalysts, which enhance the photocatalytic degradation performance of BPA from three aspects: enhanced light absorption, increased active sites, and suppression of carrier recombination [40]. The optimized BCQDs/BMO sample completely degraded BPA within 120 min under simulated sunlight, while the control sample of undoped CQDs/BMO only degraded about 84% of BPA within 160 min.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a novel 0D carbon nanomaterial, which is derived from graphite or graphene oxide, typically with no more than five layers in a diameter less than 10 nm. GQDs usually have amorphous and crystalline carbon nuclei inside, with different oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. As a member of the carbon dot family, GQDs may be more effective in catalysis than other QDs due to their graphene lattice and unique edge and quantum confinement effects within the dots. For example, Lei et al. [41] prepared GQDs by alkaline-catalyzed aqueous phase molecular fusion method and combined them with AgNO3 nanoribbons via hydrothermal method to form GQDs/AgNO3 composite materials. Under visible light irradiation, the degradation rate of ibuprofen in the 3 wt%-GQD/AgVO3 composite exceeded 90% within 120 min, while the degradation efficiency of other photocatalysts under the same conditions was relatively low. Xiong et al. [42] designed and prepared GQDs/BiWO6–x catalyst co-modified with surface oxygen vacancies and GQDs. It exhibits enhanced photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CO with a yield of 43.9 μmol g−1 h−1, which is 1.7 times higher than that of pure Bi2WO6. These preliminary studies indicate that GQDs can greatly enhance the catalytic performance of Bi2WO6 or Bi2MoO6. However, to our knowledge, there are currently few studies on the degradation of BPA using GQDs/BMO nanomaterials.

In this work, we attempted to construct GQDs/BMO composite photocatalysts by attaching GQDs onto the petals of BMO nanoflowers using a simple hydrothermal and sintering method. Loading GQDs onto BMO nanosheets can achieve effective photo-induced interfacial charge transfer by utilizing tight surface contacts. Therefore, compared with pure phase BMO, the photocatalytic activity of this composite shows a significant enhancement in the degradation of BPA under visible light irradiation. The simulated pollutants (60 mL) consisting of BPA (20 mg/L) are exposed to a 300 W xenon lamp with a photocatalyst input of 20 mg. The optimized GQDs/BMO composite catalyst degrades BPA by over 95% in 120 min under visible light, while pure BMO only achieves about 45% in the same time period. The enhanced photocatalytic activity was discussed in detail based on structural characterization, morphology observation, optical properties, and photoelectrochemical measurements, and the important role of GQDs was studied. This work opens up new possibilities for developing efficient and stable GQDs based novel photocatalysts through a simple method.

The XRD patterns of GQDs, GQDs/BMO hybrids with different composite ratios are shown in Fig. 1 (a and b), respectively. There is a clear peak at 21.38° belonging to GQDs, which is caused by the (002) lattice spacing of the carbon based material [43]. The broadening phenomenon of this peak indicates its obvious amorphous nature (Fig. 1(a)). In Fig. 1(b), several main diffraction peaks located at 10.96°, 28.31°, 32.67°, 46.83°, and 55.56° can be indexed to the (020), (131), (002), (202), and (331) crystal planes of orthogonal phase BMO (JCPDS card No. 76–2388) [44]. The XRD pattern of the GQDs/BMO hybrid prepared shows that the sample crystallizes well, and the crystal phase of BMO does not change with the attachment of GQDs on its surface. The introduction of a small amount of GQDs is an important condition for maintaining lattice integrity and implementing modification. Fig. 1(c) shows the room temperature Raman spectra of pure BMO and GQDs/BMO hybrid with 6 wt% GQDs loading. The peak group at 797 cm−1 is caused by the (a)symmetric stretching of the MoO6 octahedron in BMO, while the Raman band observed at 351 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the Bi–O bond [45–47]. These bands, as well as weak bands located at 139 and 995 cm−1, are unique features of BMO nanomaterials. Unlike pure BMO, the G6/BMO composite exhibits two additional peaks at 1600 and 1367 cm−1, which are the G and D bands with fingerprint properties [48–50], corresponding to disordered or defective regions and sp2 carbon networks in the carbon material, respectively.

In order to further investigate the atomic structure of GQDs/BMO composite materials, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was used to measure and analyze the experimental samples. As shown in Fig. 1(d), the peaks at 565, 730, 1372, and 1590 cm−1 are attributed to M–O, Bi–O stretching vibrations, Mo–O–Mo bridging stretching, and H2O molecular vibration [42], respectively. No specific adsorption peaks from the surface functional groups of GQDs particles were observed in the infrared spectrum of the composite material. The reason may be that these vibration modes are mainly concentrated in the 1200–1500 cm−1 region [41], and weak signals are drowned out by excessively high backgrounds.

Fig. 2 shows SEM images of pure BMO and G6/BMO composite with GQDs mass percentage of 6%. As shown in Fig. 2(a), pure BMO appears in the form of microspheres with a diameter of approximately 1 μm. This flower-like microsphere is actually self-assembled from two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets with a thickness of 20–30 nm (Fig. 2(b)). Due to the modification of GQDs, the size of G6/BMO is much larger than that of pure BMO (Fig. 2(c)). The floral structure ensures the maximum adhesion of GQDs, allowing them to be evenly dispersed on the BMO nano petals with size of around 10 nm (Fig. 2(d)).

The TEM images of G6/BMO are shown in Fig. 3 (a–c). Fig. 3(a) clearly presents a spherical appearance with a diameter greater than 1 μm, which is completely consistent with SEM observations. The microspheres exhibit a hierarchical flower-like morphology with sharp particle edges on the surface (Fig. 3(b)), which is the result of random rearrangement of BMO nanosheets. HRTEM (Fig. 3(c)) shows an interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm, consistent with the d-spacing of graphene (100) planes [43], and the d-spacing of BMO (131) plane was 0.306 nm [40,51], further confirming the formation of GQDs/BMO composites. From the elemental mapping images (Fig. 3(d)), it can be seen that B, Mo, and O from BMO, as well as a small amount of C from GQDs, are uniformly dispersed on the GQDs/BMO samples.

The surface chemical composition of pure BMO and G6/BMO composite materials was analyzed using XPS, and the high-resolution XPS spectra of individual elements are shown in Fig. 4 . As depicted in Fig. 4(a), the C 1s core level spectrum of BMO only shows two peaks at binding energies of 284.8 and 288.1 eV, corresponding to C–C bonds originating from contaminated amorphous carbon in BMO and C–O bonds of surface adsorbates [51–53]. However, in addition to the two peaks at the aforementioned positions, G6/BMO also has a third peak located at 286 eV, attributed to C–OH in C=O [39,54,55]. The presence of C–OH confirms the existence of GQDs in the composite photocatalyst. The asymmetric O 1s peak of BMO (Fig. 4(b)) can be decomposed into two peaks, attributed to lattice oxygen (Bi/M–O) and surface adsorbed oxygen species (hydroxyl, H2O, etc.) [56]. The O 1s binding energy of GQDs/BMO (530.6 eV) has shifted downwards by 0.5 eV compared to pure BMO. This transformation can be attributed to the change in electron density around the O atoms adsorbed on the surface after the introduction of GQDs [57]. Fig. 4(c and d) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d and Bi 4f for BMO and G6/BMO, respectively. A symmetrical peak shape implies a single valence state of an element. The deconvolution of the Mo 3d spectrum revealed peaks at binding energies of 232.2 and 235.3 eV, corresponding to the +6 oxidation state of Mo [58]. The peaks observed at 158.9 and 164.3 eV correspond to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 species, respectively, confirming the trivalent electronic state of Bi [45,59]. For BMO and G6/BMO samples, the atomic percentages of Bi:Mo element are approximately 2.19:1 and 1.94:1, respectively, which correspond to the element proportion of Bi2MoO6 in the compound.

UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was used to determine the light absorption of BMO and GQDs/BMO series samples, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The absorption edge of pure BMO is located around 460 nm, attributed to the transition of electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) under photoexcitation. After modifying GQDs on BMO nano petals, the absorption of the composite in the visible light region (400–700 nm) significantly increased. The light absorption caused by the narrow bandgap effect (1.82 eV) of GQDs can excite more photo-generated carriers in the GQDs/BMO system to purify pollutants [41]. Compared with other GQDs/BMO, G6/BMO exhibits the highest absorption rate in the 350–560 nm range. The bandgap (E g) of BMO or GQDs/BMO is calculated based on the Tauc plot (the inset in Fig. 5(a)), where the linear segment is extrapolated to the x-axis to obtain the bandgap of the material. The E g of BMO is 2.73 eV, which is consistent with the values reported in the literature [60]. After loading GQDs, the bandgap becomes smaller due to the red shift of the absorption edge, and the larger the loading amount, the smaller the bandgap.

Fig. 5(b) shows the PL spectra of BMO and GQDs/BMO composite samples at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Their peak shapes are similar with a broadened and asymmetric peak at 436 nm, but the peak intensities are different. The emission intensity of BMO modified with GQDs is significantly reduced, and G6/BMO exhibits the greatest suppression of emission. This is because the addition of GQDs greatly suppresses the recombination of electrons and holes [58], which is supported by measurements of photocurrent response and EIS. According to the light response measurement (Fig. 5(c)), the photocurrent density observed in the GQDs/BMO composite photocatalyst is much higher than that of pure BMO, indicating rapid charge transfer at the interface. The G6/BMO composite with a GQDs mass fraction of 6% has the highest photocurrent density, with a peak photocurrent density 31.5 times that of pure BMO. The semicircle with a diameter along the x-axis in the Nyquist plot is related to the charge transfer resistance in the catalyst system [61,62] (Fig. 5(d)). GQDs/BMO photoelectrodes exhibit smaller arc radii, indicating a higher charge transfer rate in the system. The equivalent circuit model is illustrated in the inset of Fig. 5(d), where R s, R ct, CPE and ZW represent the electrolyte resistance, charge transfer resistance, constant phase element CPE at the photocatalyst/electrolyte interface, and Warburg impedance (ZW) corresponding to the ion diffusion process in the electrolyte, respectively. The interface transfer resistance of G6/BMO was fitted using ZsimpWin software, which is 53.4 Ω, approximately one twentieth of that of pure BMO, indicating that the recombination of photo-induced electrons and holes in G6/BMO is more effectively suppressed. The observed charge transfer trend in the prepared photoelectrode is BMO < G5/BMO < G7/BMO < G6/BMO, completely consistent with the results of PL and photocurrent density measurements. The clear conclusion drawn from this is that the addition of GQDs significantly reduces the interfacial transfer resistance and prolongs the lifetime of electron-hole pairs, which is beneficial for the photocatalytic process.

In order to investigate the potential of the prepared material as a photocatalyst, the catalytic performance of GQDs/BMO with different GQDs contents on BPA photodegradation was tested under visible light and simulated sunlight irradiation. The degradation curve under visible light irradiation in Fig. 6 (a) clearly indicates that the activity of GQDs/BMO composite is higher than that of pure BMO nanosheets. The photocatalytic activity increased with the increase of GQDs content, reaching its maximum value at a GQDs content of 6 wt%, and then began to decrease. The photocatalytic degradation rate of BPA by G6/BMO exceeded 95% within 120 min, while under the same conditions, the degradation efficiency of pure BMO was only 45%, indicating a higher visible light photocatalytic degradation ability obtained by loading appropriate GQDs in G6/BMO. The Langmiur-Hinshelwood model was used to fit the degradation data, with the results shown in Fig. 6(b). The linear variation of ln(C 0/C)-t curve indicates that the degradation of BPA solution follows the first-order reaction kinetics. The kinetic constant k (0.2793 min−1) of G6/BMO is approximately 4.6 times that of BMO (0.0061 min−1), indicating the introduction of GQDs helps to improve the degradation efficiency of the composite material. In order to verify the visible light response of the current composite photocatalyst, a filter with a cut-off wavelength of 400 nm was unloaded to measure the degradation performance of the current catalyst under simulated sunlight and compare it with the photocatalytic performance under visible light. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the degradation rate under full spectrum has increased, but the magnitude of the increase is not significant. Especially for the G6/BMO catalyst with optimal loading, the degradation rates under the two light conditions are very close, indicating its strong visible light catalytic activity.

The current G6/BMO catalyst also exhibits good cycling stability in BPA photocatalytic degradation under visible light for four consecutive cycles (140 min per cycle). Fig. 6(d) clearly indicates that no significant deactivation occurred during repeated use. After four cycles, the degradation rate of BPA remained above 95%, indicating that G6/BMO has high photostability. As shown in Fig. 6(e), the XRD before and after the cycle is basically the same, which suggestsgood structural stability for the catalyst in the cycle. Another factor inhibiting the usability of metal-based photocatalysts during application is photo-corrosion and the subsequent dissolution of metal ions, which can lead to secondary pollution problems. For G6/BMO, if excessive BPA is added to the medium, the amount of bismuth and molybdenum is almost undetectable after 48 h of visible light irradiation, which confirms that the dissolution of photocatalysts is caused by photoelectrochemical processes involving charge carriers, rather than pure chemical processes.

The photocatalytic results showed that the BMO microspheres doped with GQDs exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of BPA. To further explore the enhancement effects caused by GQDs, besides the factors of enhanced light absorption and carrier recombination suppression, it is also necessary to analyze the free radicals involved in catalytic reactions. In order to verify the key roles of superoxide (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) in BPA degradation, experimental studies were conducted on the BPA degradation of G6/BMO at different pH values. As shown in Fig. 7(a) , BPA hardly degrades in acidic environments, because at low pH values, the solution contains a large amount of H+, which can quench superoxide and hydroxide ions and inhibit the formation of hydroxyl groups [63]. BPA has a better degradation effect in neutral and weakly alkaline environments. When pH = 9, BPA is completely degraded within 60 min, surpassing the degradation rate of 73% in neutral environments. The important roles played by superoxide ions and hydroxyl radicals in BPA degradation are also supported by sacrificial agent experiments and ESR measurements, and isopropyl alcohol was used as a typical scavenging agent for ·OH [64]. BQ and IPA were used as capture agents to remove superoxide ions (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) [65–67], and the main active substances of G6/BMO in the degradation of BPA were analyzed (Fig. 7(b)). The results showed that the addition of BQ and IPA significantly reduced the degradation efficiency of BPA, confirming that ·O2 − and ·OH are the main active substances in the BPA degradation process [68]. ESR testing is used to further verify the generation and role of superoxide and hydroxyl groups in the degradation process of BPA. As depicted in Fig. 7(c), under dark conditions, the signals of DMPO-·O2 − and DMPO-·OH are both zero, implying almost no ·O2− and ·OH in the catalyst solution in the absence of light. After irradiation, both ESR signals detected with considerable intensity indicate the presence of ·O2− and ·OH, which is consistent with the free radical capture experiment [69,70].

The positions of CB and VB in semiconductor photocatalytic materials determine the photocatalytic activity of semiconductor photocatalysts in photo oxidation and photo reduction. To this end, MS testing is used to determine the edge position of BMO and analyze the matching relationship between GQDs and BMO. As shown in Fig. 7(d), the MS curves of BMO exhibit positive slopes, indicating it is an n-type semiconductor. For n-type semiconductors, CB is usually close to its flat band potential [71,72], so the CB potential of BMO is −0.65 V vs. RHE. Combining bandgap data and using the equation E VB = E g + E CB [73], the VB potential of BMO is calculated to be 2.0 V vs. RHE. Based on the above analysis, we propose a possible mechanism for BPA degradation. As shown in Fig. 8 , under visible light irradiation, sufficient absorption of light by G6/BMO can generate a large number of e−/h+ pairs. The GQDs embellished on the surface of BMO have a dual function of capturing electrons and providing active sites, which enables the rapid migration of photo-generated e− from the semiconductor interior to the surface and the absorption by GQDs, effectively separating photo-generated e−/h+ pairs. The e− that migrates to the surface can react with adsorbed O2 and reduce it to produce ·O2 −. In addition, the positive VB position allows h+ to oxidize OH− to generate ·OH [74]. ·O2 − and ·OH can both participate in the degradation of BPA and act as key active substances.

In summary, a series of GQDs/BMO composite photocatalysts with different GQDs contents were successfully prepared through hydrothermal and sintering processes. GQDs have a size of approximately 10 nm and are evenly dispersed on the petals of BMO nanospheres with a scale of around 1 μm. The photocatalytic activity of GQDs/BMO depends on the content of GQDs in the composite, and the optimal activity can be obtained when loaded at 6 wt% (G6/BMO). Most importantly, the novel supported catalyst prepared in this study has excellent repeatability. The photocatalytic reaction follows pseudo-first order kinetics. The excellent photocatalytic performance of G6/BMO composite material is attributed to the perfect interface contact between the two components and the transfer of photo-generated electrons from BMO nanosheet to GQDs. According to the results of free radical capture and ESR measurements, both superoxide ions and hydroxyl radicals are key factors in BPA. The current work provides new insights for the development of composite photocatalysts based on GQDs.

The drugs used for preparing samples and conducting sacrificial agent experiments include bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), ammonium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), citric acid (C6H8O7), benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2), and anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O). These chemical reagents, except for anhydrous ethanol from China National Pharmaceutical Group, were purchased from Shanghai McLean Chemical Reagent Company. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade or above and used without further purification.

BMO microsphere nanostructures were synthesized using hydrothermal method. 0.477 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.119 g of Na2MoO4·2H2O were dissolved in 10 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred vigorously at room temperature to obtain a mixed solution. The above solution was sonicated for 30 min, then 60 mL of C2H6O was added dropwise, followed by sonication for 10 min and thorough mixing for 2 h. The completely dissolved solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 24 h and then naturally cooled to room temperature. After filtering through filter paper, the solid part of the product was washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and then dried at 80 °C to obtain BMO light yellow powder.

The synthesis of GQDs involves calcination and dialysis [43]. Specifically, 2 g of citric acid was placed in a crucible and heated directly to 200 °C in a muffle furnace. After 120 min, citric acid turned into a brownish black liquid and was melted into GQDs aqueous solution by adding deionized water. Then, the aqueous solution was dialyzed using dialysis method. Finally, the obtained GQD suspension was subjected to freeze-drying in a freeze dryer to obtain GQD solids.

The preparation of GQDs/BMO composite catalyst involves adding 0.2 g of BMO to 30 mL of GQDs solution (4 mg/mL) and stirring at room temperature for 1 h to obtain a uniform suspension. Afterwards, the suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 120 °C for 6 h. The obtained solid was washed alternately with deionized water and ethanol several times and dried at 80 °C to obtain a GQDs/BMO composite with 6 wt%-GQDs loading (G6/BMO). The above steps were repeated and GQDs/BMO composites with different weight percentages were synthesized using solutions of different concentrations of GODs, including G5/BMO and G7/BMO nanocomposites.

The crystal structure and microstructure of the prepared samples were characterized and analyzed using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Rigaku) with Cu-Kα radiation of λ = 0.15406 nm and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM2100UHR), respectively. The morphology was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Supra 40). The laser confocal high-resolution Raman spectrometer (Renishaw in Via plus) was used to measure Raman spectra for analyzing the local structural characteristics of samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is one of the important techniques for analyzing the surface chemistry of materials and the instrument used was a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. The FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet, 6700) was used to detect the molecular groups and surface adsorbents of catalyst samples. UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained by UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-2600) using BaSO4 powder as a reference material. ESR signals were collected in a BRUKE EMXPLUS spectrometer under dark and light conditions using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap.

Degradation experiments were conducted on BPA (20 mg/L, 60 mL) solution under simulated visible light conditions (λ ≥ 400 nm) to evaluate the catalytic performance of GQDs/BMO series catalysts. 50 mg of catalyst was sonicated in BPA solution for 7 min, and then magnetically stirred for 60 min in a dark environment to achieve desorption-adsorption equilibrium. A 300 W xenon lamp was used as the analogue light source. During irradiation, 4 mL of solution was removed every 20 min into a centrifuge tube. Degradation measurement was carried out on a UV-vis spectrophotometer. During the measurement, a quartz tube was filled with deionized water, the baseline was scanned, and the supernatant was sequentially added for detection. It was necessary to identify whether false peaks appeared in the measured full spectrum and recorded the Abs values of each sample for further analysis. When testing solutions with different pH values, the BPA solution was titrated to the desired pH value (pH = 5, 7, 9) using a 2 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution added, and then the same steps as above were followed.

The photoelectrochemical tests were performed on an electrochemical analyzer (CHI650B) using a three-electrode system. The mixed solution consisting of 15 mg catalyst, 1.5 mL isopropyl alcohol, 32 μL Nafion and a certain amount of deionized water was sonicated for 1 h and magnetically stirred overnight, and then the mixture was evenly coated on 2.0 cm × 1.0 cm fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass. FTO glass coated with catalyst, platinum plate, and Ag/AgCl were used as working electrode, counter electrode and electric reference electrode, respectively. The electrolyte was 2.5208 g Na2SO3 solution. The details for measurements of photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (MS) curves can be found elsewhere [30].

Xin Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Changzhao Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Qishen Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kai Dai: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
